Media Description:
11 promises every Engineering Manager should make (and actually keep):

After 9+ years of leading engineers, here are the promises I make to everyone who joins my team:

1. I won?t cancel our 1:1s. 
Even when things are chaotic.

2. I?ll care about your life, not just your output. 
That means asking about your family, your hobbies, and your energy.

3. When it?s time to move on, I?ll not hold you back. 
Even if it'll be a big headache for me :) 

4. I?ll always want to hear what?s broken - and how you?d fix it. 

5. You?ll never be shut down for asking ?why.? 
Good questions are part of the job.

6. You won?t just build things - you?ll know why they matter. 
We connect our work to real outcomes.

7. You won?t need to guess what matters. 
And what is expected of you. I?ll make priorities and expectations clear.

8. I?ll openly share my own mistakes. 
So you know it?s safe to take risks.

9. You?ll get the credit. 
I'll work hard to ensure your impact is also noticed outside our team.

10. You?ll talk to customers and stakeholders. 
I?ll help you grow beyond just writing code.

11. You?ll get real ownership. 
And I?ll back your decisions when needed.

What promise did I miss?

Media Description:
7 game-changing mental models for engineering managers:

?. ?????????

Invert the problem to look at the opposite perspective. List all the things that should be avoided.

For example: Instead of thinking about how to cooperate with other teams better, focus on things that should be done to make it worse (and then invert).

?. ???????

Your tendency to resist changes and keep doing things in the same way. Think where it hurts you

For example:
You don?t have team meetings because you just never did.

You don?t try to play with AI tools because you like your old code editor.

?. ??????-????? ????????

Consider more than just one immediate result of your decisions.? 

Ask ?And then what??

For example: You need to push feature for a demo. and decide to do it fast, without tests:
First-order consequences: it works during the demo.
Second-order consequences: different parts of the app break and the demo is ruined.

?. ???????

Every system you have will eventually degrade if you don?t take action.

For example: 
-Your codebase is going to get worse and worse without proper maintenance, as its complexity increases.

-Your team trained in security measures (or any kind of best practices). 6-months later, security is all but ignored. 

?. ???????? ?????

?Never attribute to malice that which is adequately explained by stupidity.? - In other words, assume good intentions.

For example: The business team promised a feature that is impossible to deliver - do you assume they want you to fail? Or maybe they just don?t understand the app?


?. ???????????

Where does it all fail? Usually in the most vulnerable part - the bottleneck.

For example:
-There might be one great engineer who holds all the knowledge - if they leave, it could significantly slow down your work.

-You might be a bottleneck - your capacity is limited, and if everything in your team goes through you, you become the limiting factor.

?. ?????????? ??????

Activation energy is a concept drawn from chemistry, where a certain amount of energy is needed to start a reaction.

For example: 
-The onboarding process is costly and takes a lot of time to get new engineers up to speed. When activation energy is high, people are reluctant to hire more.
 
To help with this, make sure to improve the process incrementally with each new engineer on-boarded.

Media Description:
10 things I wish I was told before becoming a manager:

(and would have saved me a lot of pain)

1. Don't ignore bad behaviors. You'll always regret it.
2. You should not try to make everyone love you.
3. It's lonely. You'll miss being part of the team.
4. You'll feel completely overwhelmed. It's ok. 
5. Not every principle is worth fighting for.
6. Systems are only good up to a point. 
7. Your manager is a critical asset. 
8. Help your peers succeed. 
9. Listen to your gut. 

And last:
10. Ask yourself every week: "Do my people enjoy coming to work every morning?". 

The transition from an engineer to a manager is HARD.

Don't beat yourself up. 

It gets better with time.

Media Description:
Stop overcomplicating Git!
99% of the time, you need only 7 commands.

Let's say you are on the master branch, and have some local configurations.

Before your work:
? git stash - to not lose them
? git pull - get remote updates
? git stash apply* - restore changes
??
Then:
? git checkout -b feature-branch
? -----DO THE WORK------
? git add . (requires a good .gitignore file)
? git commit -m "Added skeleton for feature"
? git push**

* Thanks Orel for the tip about apply vs pop
** In the first time it will fail, and suggest the fix:
git push --set-upstream origin BRANCH-NAME

THAT'S IT!

The cases where you need rebase, cherry pick, reset and all their friends - are rare.

Any command I missed?

#leadingdevelopers 
#git

Media Description:
Do you want to enjoy scrolling your LinkedIn feed?

Are you tired of "I'm happy to share!...", and "We are hiring"?

A couple of months ago, I complained to Sivan Helfer that I was spending too much time scrolling my LinkedIn feed. 

He was wondering how it could be more interesting than Twitter, Reddit, Facebook, and Instagram. 

All he saw was:
? People changing jobs
? People looking for jobs
? People trying to hire
 
My feed is full of interesting insights about:
? Tech
? Startups
? Leadership & Management
? Many other topics - marketing, sales, branding.

??????? content, that I rarely see in other social networks. 

To help you improve your feed, and to celebrate our Country's birthday, I prepared a list of 8 Blue-and-White LinkedIn writers that every IL developer needs to follow!

? Avi Lewis 
? Michael Parkadze 
? Igal Beilin 
? Itzy Sabo 
? Adar Kreiner 
? Gal Aga 
? Assaf Schwartz 
? Avi Romano 

Who are the other local creators you enjoy reading?

I have tons of space in my feed...
